nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; generally observed in small groups along reef edges and rubble patches, with juveniles tending toward shallower depths

diet.notes: Generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp and brine shrimp, along with high-quality marine flake and pellet foods. Feeding 2–3 small portions per day has been recommended to support its active metabolism.

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 1024x768; sharp, vibrant lateral view of a live individual clearly showing the diagnostic orange-yellow dorsal band over a bright blue body with pink face against a dark background.
Justin Philbois; 2048x1536; in-situ reef shot showing the blue body and yellow dorsal band, but distant and color-washed by the water — filler only.

summary: A vividly colored, peaceful fairy wrasse from Indonesia with a distinctive orange-to-yellow dorsal marking; generally considered reef-safe and well suited to mature community reef aquariums.
careNotes: The Orange-Back Fairy Wrasse (Cirrhilabrus aurantidorsalis) is endemic to Indonesia in the Western Central Pacific, where it has been documented occurring in small groups along reef edges and rubble patches at depths of roughly 10–25 m. Like other fairy wrasses, the Orange-Back Fairy Wrasse is known to jump, and a tight-fitting lid or eurobrace is strongly advised for any aquarium housing this species. Coloration is reported to intensify markedly during courtship displays, with males showing a more vivid orange back and a dark-red crown-like marking on the head. It is generally recommended to keep this species as a single male or as one male introduced alongside one or more females at the same time; adding females after the male is already established may provoke aggression. The Orange-Back Fairy Wrasse is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning individuals begin life as females and the dominant fish in a group transitions to male. Aggression between males is typical of the genus, and only one male per tank is generally recommended unless the aquarium is large enough to allow separate territories to form. The species is commonly considered reef-safe and is generally regarded as unlikely to harm corals or ornamental invertebrates.
breedingNotes: Captive spawning is rarely documented. Providing a harem group (one male, two or more females) in a sufficiently large aquarium may encourage nuptial display behavior.
